source.title: Mechanisms of synaptic transmission dysregulation in the prefrontal cortex: pathophysiological implications

confidence_rationale: Comprehensive mechanistic review of PFC synaptic transmission covering serotonin receptor subtypes (5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, 5-HT1A), stress hormone effects on PFC pyramidal neurons, and working memory circuitry. Provides the receptor-level anatomy for how DRN serotonin modulates the recurrent excitation underlying working memory maintenance in PFC -- the synaptic substrate for MECH-188's z_goal persistence mechanism. Confidence bounded by breadth (not a targeted MECH-188 test) and the stress-impairment framing (chronic stress degrades PFC function, which is consistent with MECH-188 failure but does not directly test top-down goal maintenance).
failure_signatures: 5-HT2A receptors on PFC layer V pyramidal neurons suppress NMDAR function via microtubule/kinesin transport -- this is the anatomical substrate for serotonin gating PFC-to-striatum output
Chronic stress impairs dendritic branching and spine density in PFC pyramidal neurons -- predicts that chronic harm exposure degrades MECH-188 substrate, not just activates it

Acute stress enhances glutamatergic transmission in PFC transiently (GR-mediated) -- consistent with brief harm spikes being tolerable; chronic stress (analogous to sustained z_harm_a elevation) is what destroys MECH-188 capacity
PFC working memory: sustained spiking in dlPFC during delay periods arises from NMDA-mediated recurrent excitation -- this is the cellular correlate of z_goal persistence after seeding

mapping.source_claim_statement: PFC receives major serotonergic projections. 5-HT2A receptors on PFC pyramidal neurons suppress NMDAR function; 5-HT2C has opposing effects. Working memory in dlPFC is sustained by NMDA-mediated recurrent excitation during delay periods. Chronic stress impairs PFC dendritic structure and synaptic plasticity; acute stress transiently enhances glutamatergic transmission.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-188 requires a synaptic mechanism by which DRN 5-HT projections can sustain z_goal in dlPFC against harm interruption. Yan and Rein supply this at the receptor level: (1) DRN 5-HT acts on layer V pyramidal 5-HT2A receptors, modulating the NMDA-mediated recurrent excitation that is the cellular substrate of working memory maintenance; (2) the balance of 5-HT2A (inhibitory on NMDAR via kinesin) and 5-HT2C (enhancing) sets the gain of PFC persistent activity; (3) acute harm (acute stress) transiently up-regulates glutamatergic tone in PFC, which could briefly boost z_goal maintenance capacity; (4) chronic harm (chronic stress) ultimately degrades PFC structure, which is the biological basis for the clinical observation that sustained adversity eventually collapses goal-directed behaviour even with an intact 5-HT system. This maps the MECH-188 LONG_HORIZON context requirement to the chronic-vs-acute stress distinction in PFC physiology.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This review does not measure goal representation maintenance under aversive challenge directly. It provides the anatomical and receptor-level substrate but does not test whether 5-HT specifically maintains a learned goal representation when harm signals are elevated. The serotonin effects on PFC are bidirectional and subtype-dependent -- the net effect on z_goal persistence would depend on which receptor subtypes are dominant in dlPFC during harm states, which is not resolved here.
mapping.source_context: Yan lab (SUNY Buffalo) review in Molecular Psychiatry. Covers a wide scope -- the serotonin section is one of several monoaminergic sections. PMC open access. 2021 publication date makes this the most recent of the three entries.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (Mol Psychiatry, comprehensive mechanistic review). Mapping fidelity moderate -- provides the synaptic substrate but not the goal-persistence-under-harm test. Transfer risk: (1) stress paradigm is not identical to z_harm_a spike condition; (2) receptor subtype balance is not fully specified for the harm context; (3) MECH-188 specifically invokes dlPFC but this review covers mPFC and vlPFC as well, which may have different serotonin response profiles.
